texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sustainability is a commonly used terminology attempting to embrace all technological developments. Sustainable development can be applied within various fields, e.g. with respect to sustainable economy, sustainable environmental impacts, sustainable population impacts etc. Nevertheless, the true attempt on reaching sustainable development lies in the practice of satisfying all the aspects of sustainability. Many new technologies or solutions to improve existing technologies are being developed to achieve the objective of sustainable growth.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) and membrane crystallization (MCr) have been applied for treatment of real industrial wastewater to produce high quality sodium sulfate. Simultaneously, MD and MCr minimize waste disposal and generate a fresh water stream. Untreated and wastewater pre-concentrated by nanofiltration have been utilized in MD and MCr under several operative conditions. The untreated wastewater indicated the steadiest performance. The MD and MCr operations have proven their long term stability throughout the concentration of wastewater. The treatment has been continued for more than 90 h for the untreated wastewater. Despite the utilized wastewaters have a high scaling potential in particular for silica, calcium and magnesium, no reduction in flux or wetting of the membrane surface have been observed due to fouling. Moreover, the crystalline product of Na2SO4 recovered as Thenardite (the anhydrous form) exhibits narrow size distribution and low incorporation of impurities.
texts.text: Membrane distillation (MD) and membrane crystallization (MCr) have been applied for treatment of real industrial wastewater to produce high quality sodium sulfate. Simultaneously, MD and MCr minimize waste disposal and generate a fresh water stream. Untreated and wastewater pre-concentrated by nanofiltration have been utilized in MD and MCr under several operative conditions. The untreated wastewater indicated the steadiest performance. The MD and MCr operations have proven their long term stability throughout the concentration of wastewater. The treatment has been continued for more than 90 h for the untreated wastewater. Despite the utilized wastewaters have a high scaling potential in particular for silica, calcium and magnesium, no reduction in flux or wetting of the membrane surface have been observed due to fouling. Moreover, the crystalline product of Na2SO4 recovered as Thenardite (the anhydrous form) exhibits narrow size distribution and low incorporation of impurities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The very broad expression of sustainable development requires some applied guidelines which is provided by the process intensification strategy (PIS) aiming at reducing energy consumption, minimizing the use of raw materials, maximize the final product output, reducing waste, minimize environmental and human impacts, minimizing footprints etc. Membrane engineering is a new concept of significant potential in realizing the objectives of sustainable development. The very close alignment of membrane operations and process intensification strategy makes membrane engineering a part of the future technological improvements.

Membrane processes such as membrane distillation (MD) and membrane crystallization (MCr) have been evaluated for treatment of challenging solutions such as brine of reverse osmosis (RO) plants [1] and for oilfield produced water [2]. Treatment of these streams cannot only reduce/eliminate the associated hazards but also provide the opportunity to recover valuable components encountering zero liquid discharge.
texts.text: another aspect of MD and MCr is the potential of treating industrial wastewaters. Untreated industrial wastewater streams have a negative impact on environment and industrial economy. Treatment of these streams cannot only reduce/eliminate the associated hazards but also provide the opportunity to recover valuable components encountering zero liquid discharge.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MDisbasedonvaporpressure gradient (mainly temperature) created across a microporous hydrophobic membrane [3]. MCr is an extension of MD aiming to concentrate the solution above super-saturation level in order to recover the components contained in the feed solution. The advantages of using MD and MCr are the low operative temperatures and pressures, the high permeate quality independent of feed characteristics (theoretical 100% rejection of non-volatile components), the simple configuration and operation and treatment of highly concentrated solutions [3]. Unlike pressure driven membrane operations, the impact of concentration in MD and MCr is very small with respect to temperature [4]. MCr has been applied successfully for recovery of minerals from different streams [5 -8]. Furthermore, MCr has some important advantages with respect to traditional crystallization processes such as well-controlled nucleation and growth kinetics, faster crystallization rates and reduced induction time, control of super-saturation level and rate. Therefore, it is possible to target the crystal polymorph form, to obtain crystals with narrow size distribution and of high purity [9].
texts.text: MDisbasedonvaporpressure gradient (mainly temperature) created across a microporous hydrophobic membrane [3]. MCr is an extension of MD aiming to concentrate the solution above super-saturation level in order to recover the components contained in the feed solution. The advantages of using MD and MCr are the low operative temperatures and pressures, the high permeate quality independent of feed characteristics (theoretical 100% rejection of non-volatile components), the simple configuration and operation and treatment of highly concentrated solutions [3]. Unlike pressure driven membrane operations, the impact of concentration in MD and MCr is very small with respect to temperature [4]. MCr has been applied successfully for recovery of minerals from different streams [5 -8]. Furthermore, MCr has some important advantages with respect to traditional crystallization processes such as well-controlled nucleation and growth kinetics, faster crystallization rates and reduced induction time, control of super-saturation level and rate. Therefore, it is possible to target the crystal polymorph form, to obtain crystals with narrow size distribution and of high purity [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper evaluates the potential of using MD and MCr for the treatment of real industrial wastewater containing high amounts of sodium sulfate. Sodium sulfate is a valuable material used in various industries for cleaning detergents, glass, pulp and paper [10]. Traditionally, Na2SO4 is extracted from salt lake brines and through mining processes. China is the largest producer with more than 70% of world production [10]. Sodium sulfate can also be recovered as by-product from i.e. battery reclamation, cellulose and silica pigments etc. [10]. Certain industrial wastewater streams also contain significant quantities of Na2SO4. The treatment of these streams can reduce their negative impact on environment and human and, simultaneously contribute in achieving the objective of sustainable consumption of sodium sulfate and water.
texts.text: This paper evaluates the potential of using MD and MCr for the treatment of real industrial wastewater containing high amounts of sodium sulfate. Sodium sulfate is a valuable material used in various industries for cleaning detergents, glass, pulp and paper [10]. Traditionally, Na2SO4 is extracted from salt lake brines and through mining processes. China is the largest producer with more than 70% of world production [10]. Sodium sulfate can also be recovered as by-product from i.e. battery reclamation, cellulose and silica pigments etc. [10]. Certain industrial wastewater streams also contain significant quantities of Na2SO4. The treatment of these streams can reduce their negative impact on environment and human and, simultaneously contribute in achieving the objective of sustainable consumption of sodium sulfate and water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A lab-scale MD and MCr plant in direct contact configuration has been utilized in the treatment of industrial wastewater and recovery of Na2SO4. A detailed description of the plant can be found elsewhere [11]. The plant is equipped with two commercial polypropylene membrane modules from Microdyn-Nadir (Table 1).
texts.text: A lab-scale MD and MCr plant in direct contact configuration has been utilized in the treatment of industrial wastewater and recovery of Na2SO4. A detailed description of the plant can be found elsewhere [11]. The plant is equipped with two commercial polypropylene membrane modules from Microdyn-Nadir (Table 1).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. MD and MCr experiments
texts.text: 2.3. MD and MCr experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD and MCr tests have been carried out utilizing as feed the raw industrial wastewater (referred to as solution # 1 and solution # 2) and following the structure in Table 3. Different flow rates (100 and 200 L/h) and feed temperature conditions (ranging from 31 to 51 °C) have been utilized. The permeate flow rate has been kept on 100 L/h throughout the experiments.
texts.text: MD and MCr tests have been carried out utilizing as feed the raw industrial wastewater (referred to as solution # 1 and solution # 2) and following the structure in Table 3. Different flow rates (100 and 200 L/h) and feed temperature conditions (ranging from 31 to 51 °C) have been utilized. The permeate flow rate has been kept on 100 L/h throughout the experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MDandMCrtests have been performed also utilizing as feed the industrial wastewater treated with nanofiltration. These last MD and MCr experiments on nanofiltration retentate have been conducted at two different initial concentrations, i.e. at 93.46 g/l Na2SO4 (solution # 3) and 132.0 g/l Na2SO4 (solution # 4). Pretreatment with nanofiltration increases the concentration of the MD/MCr feed thus decreasing the time needed to reach sodium sulfate crystallization. Therefore, only two different MD experimental conditions have been conducted for the NF pretreated wastewater (Table 3) whereas the final crystallization processes have been carried out by using 200 L/h as feed flow rate and around 37 °C as feed temperature.
texts.text: MDandMCrtests have been performed also utilizing as feed the industrial wastewater treated with nanofiltration. These last MD and MCr experiments on nanofiltration retentate have been conducted at two different initial concentrations, i.e. at 93.46 g/l Na2SO4 (solution # 3) and 132.0 g/l Na2SO4 (solution # 4). Pretreatment with nanofiltration increases the concentration of the MD/MCr feed thus decreasing the time needed to reach sodium sulfate crystallization. Therefore, only two different MD experimental conditions have been conducted for the NF pretreated wastewater (Table 3) whereas the final crystallization processes have been carried out by using 200 L/h as feed flow rate and around 37 °C as feed temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cleaning of the membrane has been performed after each 10 h of experiment with distillate water for 1 h. Moreover, the membrane has been further cleaned after crystallization with a citric acid solution adjusted to pH = 3 -4. Cleaning with citric acid has been carried out for 1 h followed by extensive rinsing with distillate water for 1 h.
texts.text: Cleaning of the membrane has been performed after each 10 h of experiment with distillate water for 1 h. Moreover, the membrane has been further cleaned after crystallization with a citric acid solution adjusted to pH = 3 -4. Cleaning with citric acid has been carried out for 1 h followed by extensive rinsing with distillate water for 1 h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this study was to produce two streams: (i) a high quality water stream and (ii) a solid stream of Na2SO4. The quality of permeate was scrutinized in terms of electrical conductivity. For what concerns the solid stream of Na2SO4, the produced crystals were visually observed through optical microscope and characterized by optical microscope, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analyses. A sample of the mother liquid containing crystals has been analyzed by microscope when the first crystals have been observed in the solution. Evolution of the crystals in time is estimated by analyzing samples after 30 and 60 min, respectively. The microscopic pictures are used for judgment of mean diameter (dm), coefficient of variation (CV), crystal size distribution (CSD), growth (G) and nucleation rate (B0). SEM and EDX are utilized for polymorph and purity estimations.
texts.text: The aim of this study was to produce two streams: (i) a high quality water stream and (ii) a solid stream of Na2SO4. The quality of permeate was scrutinized in terms of electrical conductivity. For what concerns the solid stream of Na2SO4, the produced crystals were visually observed through optical microscope and characterized by optical microscope, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analyses. A sample of the mother liquid containing crystals has been analyzed by microscope when the first crystals have been observed in the solution. Evolution of the crystals in time is estimated by analyzing samples after 30 and 60 min, respectively. The microscopic pictures are used for judgment of mean diameter (dm), coefficient of variation (CV), crystal size distribution (CSD), growth (G) and nucleation rate (B0). SEM and EDX are utilized for polymorph and purity estimations.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Potential scaling studies
texts.text: 3.1. Potential scaling studies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Silica and calcium are high risk scaling components. Potential of silica to form complexes with various metals makes the associated fouling further severe. Silica can create complexes with Fe, Al, Ca, Mg, and Mn [13,14]. In literature silica scaling is mainly related to reverse osmosis and only few articles are available for membrane distillation [15]. In reverse osmosis, scaling is normally avoided by adding proper antiscalants (mainly borate is used [16]) before the membrane operation. However, chemically treatment is not aligned with the process intensification strategy and the sustainable development, therefore to control the scaling phenomena is of great interests. Not only silica can create problems in MD and MCr but also calcium sulfates and calcium carbonates. In literature [17] it has been proved that calcium can be removed from the feed solution as CaSO4 through chemical treatment with Na2CO3. Nevertheless, in this study, the wastewater has not been treated chemically prior or during MD/MCr.
texts.text: Silica and calcium are high risk scaling components. Potential of silica to form complexes with various metals makes the associated fouling further severe. Silica can create complexes with Fe, Al, Ca, Mg, and Mn [13,14]. In literature silica scaling is mainly related to reverse osmosis and only few articles are available for membrane distillation [15]. In reverse osmosis, scaling is normally avoided by adding proper antiscalants (mainly borate is used [16]) before the membrane operation. However, chemically treatment is not aligned with the process intensification strategy and the sustainable development, therefore to control the scaling phenomena is of great interests. Not only silica can create problems in MD and MCr but also calcium sulfates and calcium carbonates. In literature [17] it has been proved that calcium can be removed from the feed solution as CaSO4 through chemical treatment with Na2CO3. Nevertheless, in this study, the wastewater has not been treated chemically prior or during MD/MCr.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At time of Na2SO4 crystallization, silica concentration has reached above 700 ppm, much higher than its solubility (186 mg/L at 40 °C [14]). Similar, the concentration of calcium reach above 400 ppm, which also causes severe risk of gypsum scaling. Despite that, the silica and gypsum concentration has exceeded their solubility, no decline of flux has been observed for solutions # 1 and # 2 and therefore no particular pore blocking. Moreover, the conductivity of permeate has been measured frequently during the tests in order to detect eventually wetting of the membrane. In all the carried out tests, permeate conductivity at the end of each test was lower with respect to its value at the start of the experiment. This demonstrated that the intrusion of feed solution through the membrane pores was negligible. Therefore, polypropylene membranes preserved the crucial requisite of hydrophobicity during the operative time of these experiments. The reason of no observation of silica scaling can be related to induction periods. Previous studies on silica scaling in MD [15] proved that starting with a concentration of 300 mg/L of silica, an induction period of 8.2 h is required to achieve scaling. Nevertheless, the MD/MCr treatment has been carried out for 92, 90, 28 and 22 h for solutions # 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively, thus the membranes have proven their long term stability and it must be expected that induction times have been exceeded. Moreover, not any particular cleaning has been carried out to minimize scaling. Beside distillate water, only cleaning with citric acid for 1 h followed by rinsing with distillate water was utilized. The cleaning procedure has only been performed after precipitation of Na2SO4, that means before beginning of experimentation with a new solution.
texts.text: At time of Na2SO4 crystallization, silica concentration has reached above 700 ppm, much higher than its solubility (186 mg/L at 40 °C [14]). Similar, the concentration of calcium reach above 400 ppm, which also causes severe risk of gypsum scaling. Despite that, the silica and gypsum concentration has exceeded their solubility, no decline of flux has been observed for solutions # 1 and # 2 and therefore no particular pore blocking. Moreover, the conductivity of permeate has been measured frequently during the tests in order to detect eventually wetting of the membrane. In all the carried out tests, permeate conductivity at the end of each test was lower with respect to its value at the start of the experiment. This demonstrated that the intrusion of feed solution through the membrane pores was negligible. Therefore, polypropylene membranes preserved the crucial requisite of hydrophobicity during the operative time of these experiments. The reason of no observation of silica scaling can be related to induction periods. Previous studies on silica scaling in MD [15] proved that starting with a concentration of 300 mg/L of silica, an induction period of 8.2 h is required to achieve scaling. Nevertheless, the MD/MCr treatment has been carried out for 92, 90, 28 and 22 h for solutions # 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively, thus the membranes have proven their long term stability and it must be expected that induction times have been exceeded. Moreover, not any particular cleaning has been carried out to minimize scaling. Beside distillate water, only cleaning with citric acid for 1 h followed by rinsing with distillate water was utilized. The cleaning procedure has only been performed after precipitation of Na2SO4, that means before beginning of experimentation with a new solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For all the conducted experimental tests, pH has been measured regularly and its value has never been exceeding 5, contradicting with the decrease in solubility of amorphous silica with decrease in pH and increase in temperature [18].
texts.text: For all the conducted experimental tests, pH has been measured regularly and its value has never been exceeding 5, contradicting with the decrease in solubility of amorphous silica with decrease in pH and increase in temperature [18].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Crystallization of sodium sulfate
texts.text: 3.2. Crystallization of sodium sulfate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In terms of concentrations, precipitation of Na2SO4 has been observed at concentrations around 370.3 g/L for solutions # 1 and # 2 (Fig. 4), 318.4 and 319.4 g/L for the retentate of nanofiltration.
texts.text: In terms of concentrations, precipitation of Na2SO4 has been observed at concentrations around 370.3 g/L for solutions # 1 and # 2 (Fig. 4), 318.4 and 319.4 g/L for the retentate of nanofiltration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sodium sulfate crystals produced from solution # 2 have been characterized according to different parameters. As above described, after nucleation, suspension samples have been withdrawn from the retentate tank every 30 min, particles filtered and examined visually (via an optic microscope) in order to determine crystals shape, dimension and crystal size distribution. Knowledge of the evolution of particle size distribution as function of time allows to evaluate quality, mean diameter (dm), coefficient of variation (CV) and growth rate of the produced crystals.
texts.text: Sodium sulfate crystals produced from solution # 2 have been characterized according to different parameters. As above described, after nucleation, suspension samples have been withdrawn from the retentate tank every 30 min, particles filtered and examined visually (via an optic microscope) in order to determine crystals shape, dimension and crystal size distribution. Knowledge of the evolution of particle size distribution as function of time allows to evaluate quality, mean diameter (dm), coefficient of variation (CV) and growth rate of the produced crystals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Illustration of CSD and data of crystal characterization are reported in Fig. 6 and Table 4, respectively. CSDs of the samples are in general narrow also confirmed by CV below 50% for all the samples. CV is an industrially relevant parameter since it measures the scatterings of crystal product size around its mean. The CV of ideal mixed-suspension crystallizer, widely employed in the industrial crystallization, is 50% for size-independent growth, but becomes significantly higher for size-dependent growth [20] Low CV is characteristic of a narrow crystal size distribution and, therefore, of a better product [9]. This trend can be attributed to a secondary nucleation and crystal growth which occur simultaneously due to the contact of the growing crystals with different parts of the plant. Overall the results indicate the potential of keeping the crystallization process of sodium sulfate steady and of good quality by utilizing MCr.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. SEM and EDX analysis
texts.text: 3.3. SEM and EDX analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recovered Na2SO4 crystals from all the wastewater solutions have been analyzed by SEM and EDX to estimate polymorphic structure. Sodium sulfate exists in different hydrate forms including the anhydrous form (Thenardite), heptahydrate and decahydrate (Mirabilite). However, the stable forms are considered as Thenardite and Mirabilite with transition around 33 wt.% Na2SO4 and a temperature of 32 °C [21]. However, addition of NaCl up to 15 wt.% decreases the transition temperature to 25 °C as a consequence of decreased Na2SO4 -water interactions [22].
texts.text: The recovered Na2SO4 crystals from all the wastewater solutions have been analyzed by SEM and EDX to estimate polymorphic structure. Sodium sulfate exists in different hydrate forms including the anhydrous form (Thenardite), heptahydrate and decahydrate (Mirabilite). However, the stable forms are considered as Thenardite and Mirabilite with transition around 33 wt.% Na2SO4 and a temperature of 32 °C [21]. However, addition of NaCl up to 15 wt.% decreases the transition temperature to 25 °C as a consequence of decreased Na2SO4 -water interactions [22].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The easy control of MCr has previous shown the great potential of targeting the desired polymorphs by changing operational conditions [23 -27]. In this work the operative conditions of the MCr (in particular regarding temperature) were chosen in order to address the formation of sodium sulfate as Thenardite (as proved by the EDX analyses below reported).
texts.text: The easy control of MCr has previous shown the great potential of targeting the desired polymorphs by changing operational conditions [23 -27]. In this work the operative conditions of the MCr (in particular regarding temperature) were chosen in order to address the formation of sodium sulfate as Thenardite (as proved by the EDX analyses below reported).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The obtained SEM images for the different solutions can be found in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 at different magnifications. EDX analyses have been performed to analyze the composition of the crystals and to estimate if any impurities are present. The EDX analysis (Fig. 9) clearly shows that mainly sodium sulfate as Thenardite has been precipitate, though a very small amount of impurities mainly silicium is incorporated into the crystal lattice (0.22, 0.14, 0.20 and 0.06 wt.% from solutions # 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively). In solution # 3 and # 4, small amounts of other impurities such as calcium and magnesium have also been detected, accounted by the higher concentration of bivalent ions present in the wastewater at crystallization time due to the nanofiltration pretreatment.
texts.text: The obtained SEM images for the different solutions can be found in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 at different magnifications. EDX analyses have been performed to analyze the composition of the crystals and to estimate if any impurities are present. The EDX analysis (Fig. 9) clearly shows that mainly sodium sulfate as Thenardite has been precipitate, though a very small amount of impurities mainly silicium is incorporated into the crystal lattice (0.22, 0.14, 0.20 and 0.06 wt.% from solutions # 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively). In solution # 3 and # 4, small amounts of other impurities such as calcium and magnesium have also been detected, accounted by the higher concentration of bivalent ions present in the wastewater at crystallization time due to the nanofiltration pretreatment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Evaluation of new metrics
texts.text: 3.4. Evaluation of new metrics

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To compare easily the conventional unit operations with membrane technology new metrics have been proposed [28]. New metrics allow monitoring the progress and improvement of membrane operations in the logic of process intensification taking into account plant size, weight, flexibility, modularity etc. The impact of treatment by means of membrane crystallization (MD and MCr) has been evaluated through new metrics. In particular, mass intensity (MI) (Eq. (8)), waste intensity (WI) (Eq. (9)), productivity/size ratio (PS) (Eq. (10)) and productivity/weight ratio (PW) (Eq. (11)) have been calculated. Changes of mass andwasteintensities and productivity with respect to size of membrane (0.2 m 2 -active surface area) and weight (0.467 kg -module) have been identified. MI and WI decrease significantly with increase in water recovery factor (Fig. 10) from above 25 to below 1.5 and 0.2 for MI and WI, respectively.
texts.text: To compare easily the conventional unit operations with membrane technology new metrics have been proposed [28]. New metrics allow monitoring the progress and improvement of membrane operations in the logic of process intensification taking into account plant size, weight, flexibility, modularity etc. The impact of treatment by means of membrane crystallization (MD and MCr) has been evaluated through new metrics. In particular, mass intensity (MI) (Eq. (8)), waste intensity (WI) (Eq. (9)), productivity/size ratio (PS) (Eq. (10)) and productivity/weight ratio (PW) (Eq. (11)) have been calculated. Changes of mass andwasteintensities and productivity with respect to size of membrane (0.2 m 2 -active surface area) and weight (0.467 kg -module) have been identified. MI and WI decrease significantly with increase in water recovery factor (Fig. 10) from above 25 to below 1.5 and 0.2 for MI and WI, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PS and PW have been estimated based on membrane area and weight of module and only based on the utilized membrane system (Fig. 11). Prior to Na2SO4 saturation, PS is equal and PW is proportional to trans-membrane flux, respectively. The slight decrease in PS and PW is due to concentration increase, which decreases the driving force. The feed temperature utilized in experiment of membrane crystallization has been relatively low (around 37.4 °C), thus the productivity can be easily increased. These numbers also indicates the flexibility of a MD/MCr plant. From the time of saturation, Na2SO4 is being produced at rate of 0.43 kg/m 2 ·h (similar to that of water with PS of 0.63 kg/m 2 ·h).
texts.text: PS and PW have been estimated based on membrane area and weight of module and only based on the utilized membrane system (Fig. 11). Prior to Na2SO4 saturation, PS is equal and PW is proportional to trans-membrane flux, respectively. The slight decrease in PS and PW is due to concentration increase, which decreases the driving force. The feed temperature utilized in experiment of membrane crystallization has been relatively low (around 37.4 °C), thus the productivity can be easily increased. These numbers also indicates the flexibility of a MD/MCr plant. From the time of saturation, Na2SO4 is being produced at rate of 0.43 kg/m 2 ·h (similar to that of water with PS of 0.63 kg/m 2 ·h).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: amounts of sodium sulfate. Treatment of four different solutions has been carried out: two streams are coming directly from the industrial process (solutions # 1 and # 2) and two streams have been preconcentrated by nanofiltration prior to the MD/MCr process (solutions # 3 and # 4). The untreated industrial wastewater showed the most stable performance for more than 90 h with no decline in trans-membrane flux despite the very high scaling potential of components such as silica, calcium and magnesium. Sodium sulfate has been recovered as a high quality product in terms of narrow size distribution, low coefficient of variation, constant growth rate and low incorporation of impurities. Crystallization of wastewater preconcentrated by nanofiltration indicated a higher incorporation of impurities into the crystal lattice caused by the higher amounts of bivalent ions with respect to the unfiltered wastewater. Therefore, due the stable performance and reduced incorporation of impurities, the unfiltered wastewater might be more suitable for direct treatment by means of MD and MCr.
texts.text: amounts of sodium sulfate. Treatment of four different solutions has been carried out: two streams are coming directly from the industrial process (solutions # 1 and # 2) and two streams have been preconcentrated by nanofiltration prior to the MD/MCr process (solutions # 3 and # 4). The untreated industrial wastewater showed the most stable performance for more than 90 h with no decline in trans-membrane flux despite the very high scaling potential of components such as silica, calcium and magnesium. Sodium sulfate has been recovered as a high quality product in terms of narrow size distribution, low coefficient of variation, constant growth rate and low incorporation of impurities. Crystallization of wastewater preconcentrated by nanofiltration indicated a higher incorporation of impurities into the crystal lattice caused by the higher amounts of bivalent ions with respect to the unfiltered wastewater. Therefore, due the stable performance and reduced incorporation of impurities, the unfiltered wastewater might be more suitable for direct treatment by means of MD and MCr.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper addresses sodium sulfate recovery, however, the possibility for implementing MD and MCr in other industrial processes also exists. The potential of recycling salts or other valuable compounds from industrial waste minimize the future possibility of mineral depletion. The simultaneously water and minerals production implies the industries are moving further towards complete alignment with sustainable development.
texts.text: This paper addresses sodium sulfate recovery, however, the possibility for implementing MD and MCr in other industrial processes also exists. The potential of recycling salts or other valuable compounds from industrial waste minimize the future possibility of mineral depletion. The simultaneously water and minerals production implies the industries are moving further towards complete alignment with sustainable development.
